section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: The oxidation rate of the binary nickel-molybdenum alloy passes through a maximum for the alloy containing $15\%$ Mo, and the scale formed by the oxidation is $\mathrm{NiMoO_4}$ and NiO.

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: Upon thermal cycling from above $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to below $660^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the $\mathrm{NiMoO_4}$ undergoes a phase transformation which causes the protective scale on the oxidized metal to spall.

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: Similarly, the oxidation rate of nickel-molybdenum alloys containing chromium passes through a maximum for alloys containing between 2 and $6\%$ Cr.

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: Alloys containing more than $6\%$ Cr are insensitive to thermal cycling and the molybdenum content because the oxide scale is predominantly stable $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ .

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: An abrupt decrease, by a factor of about 40, in the oxidation rate at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is observed when the chromium content is increased from 5.9 to $6.2\%$ .

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: The oxidation resistance of INOR-8 is excellent, and continuous operation at temperatures up to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is feasible.

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: Intermittent use at temperatures as high as $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ could be tolerated.

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: For temperatures up to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the oxidation rate is not measurable; it is essentially nonexistent after $1000\mathrm{hr}$ of exposure in static air.

section: 13-5. OxIDATION RESISTANCE
text: It is estimated that oxidation of 0.001 to 0.002 in, would occur in $100,000\mathrm{hr}$ of operation at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: If graphite could be used as a moderator in direct contact with a molten salt, it would make possible a molten-salt reactor with a breeding ratio in excess of one (see Chapter 14).

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: Tests at temperatures up to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the ternary salt mixture $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ gave no indication of the decomposition of the fluoride and no gas evolution so long as the graphite was free from a silicon impurity.

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: Although these penetration tests showed no visible effects of attack of the graphite by the salt, analyses of the salt for carbon showed that at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ more than $1\%$ carbon may be picked up in $100\mathrm{hr}$ .

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: The carbon pickup appears to be sensitive to temperature, however, inasmuch as only $0.025\%$ carbon was found in the salt after a 1000-hr exposure at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: In (B) the pores are filled with salt. $(100\times)$

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: In the tests run thus far, no positive indication has been found of carburization of the nickel-alloy containers exposed to molten salts and graphite at the temperatures at present contemplated for power reactors $(< 1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ .

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: The carburization effect seems to be quite temperature sensitive, however, since tests at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ showed carburization of Hastelloy B to a depth of 0.003 in. in 500 hr of exposure to $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ containing graphite.

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: A test of Inconel and graphite in a thermal-convection loop in which the maximum bulk temperature of the fluoride salt was $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ gave a maximum carburization depth of 0.05 in. in 500 hr.

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: In this case, however, the temperature of the metal-salt interface where the carburization occurred was considerably higher than $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , probably about $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 13-8. COMPATIBILITY OF GRAPHITE WITH MOLTEN SALTS AND NICKEL-BASE ALLOYS
text: A mixture of sodium and graphite is known to be a good carburizing agent, and tests with it have confirmed the large effect of temperature on the carburization of both Inconel and INOR-8, as shown in Table 13-9.

text: Many additional tests are being performed with a variety of molten fluoride salts to measure both penetration of the graphite and carburization of INOR-8.

section: 13-9. MATERIALS FOR VALVE SEATS AND BEARING SURFACES
text: Nearly all metals, alloys, and hard-facing materials tend to undergo solid-phase bonding when held together under pressure in molten fluoride salts at temperatures above $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 13-9. MATERIALS FOR VALVE SEATS AND BEARING SURFACES
text: Screening tests in a search for nonbonding materials that will stand up under the molten salt environment have indicated that the most promising materials are TiC-Ni and WC-Co types of cermets with nickel or cobalt contents of less than 35 w/o, tungsten, and molybdenum.

section: 13-9. MATERIALS FOR VALVE SEATS AND BEARING SURFACES
text: The tests, in general, have been of less than 1000-hr duration, so the useful lives of these materials have not yet been determined.
